description:
Single-pane-of-glass overview. Open this first during any incident.

GoodData CN Service Reference:
  tiger-*          All GoodData CN services use this prefix (e.g. tiger-gateway = API Gateway)
  Calcique         Analytics Engine — computes dashboard & report queries
  Result Cache     Stores computed results to avoid re-running identical queries
  SQL Executor     Runs queries against your external data sources
  Pulsar           Internal message queue for background job processing
  Export Builder   Renders PDF/PNG exports using headless Chrome
  Hikari           Database connection pool — number of open DB connections per service

panels.description: Ratio of Ready nodes. Any value < 1.0 = node(s) NotReady — pods may be evicted or unschedulable.
panels.title: Nodes Ready

panels.description: Ratio of ready replicas across all GoodData CN deployments.
panels.title: Pods Ready

panels.description:
Ready vs desired replica counts for all Deployments and StatefulSets in the selected namespace, plus Pulsar message broker components (brokers, bookies, ZooKeeper) from pulsar-related namespaces.

STATUS COLUMN GUIDE
  🟢 100%        -- all replicas running and ready
  🔴 0%          -- no replicas running; this service is completely down
  partial %      -- service degraded, some replicas unavailable (investigate immediately)
  Scaled down    -- Desired = 0; the service was intentionally stopped, not an error
  Stuck Pending  -- pods have been waiting to be scheduled for more than 2 minutes; usually means nodes are full or a taint/toleration mismatch

Sorted by Status ascending so partially-ready and down services appear at the top.

targets.expr:
count by (namespace, deployment) (
  label_replace(
    kube_pod_status_phase{cluster_name=~"$cluster", namespace=~"$ns|$pulsar_ns", phase="Pending"} == 1
    and on(namespace, pod)
    (time() - kube_pod_created{cluster_name=~"$cluster", namespace=~"$ns|$pulsar_ns"} > 120),
    "deployment", "$1", "pod", "^(.*)-[a-z0-9]+-[a-z0-9]+$"
  )
)

panels.title: Service Readiness per Deployment

panels.description: Containers in OOMKilled state at the end of the selected time range. Set the range end to “now” to see the current count.
panels.title: OOM Kills

panels.description: Image tag of the running metadata-api container. This commit SHA identifies the exact GoodData CN build deployed in this namespace.
panels.title: GoodData-CN Version

panels.description: Start time of the most recently launched pod in this namespace — a reliable proxy for the last Helm deployment timestamp.
panels.title: Last Helm Deployment
API Request Rate by Upstream Service
panels.description:
Request rate through the API Gateway broken down by upstream service and HTTP status class.

Uses forward_call_response_status_count_total which provides per-upstream-host granularity (upstream_host label). This metric may not be available in all GoodData.CN versions.

MIGRATION NOTE: In GoodData.CN ≥ 3.58 the equivalent OTel metric is http_server_request_duration_seconds_count with labels http_route (upstream route) and http_response_status_code. If this panel shows no data, switch to that metric.

HOW TO READ THIS CHART
• X-axis = time
• Y-axis = how long the API took to respond, in milliseconds (lower = faster)
• Color = how many requests landed at that response speed. Brighter/warmer = more requests at that latency.

HEALTHY PATTERN
Nearly all activity clusters in the lower rows (< 500 ms). Short bright spikes to 1–2 s are normal when users run heavy or complex reports.

WARNING SIGNS TO WATCH FOR
▲ A warm band that drifts upward over time
   → A backend service is gradually getting slower.

▲ A sudden bright streak near the top (5 s or higher)
   → A service may be stalled, restarting, or hitting a hard timeout.

▲ The entire chart lights up at once (wide bright band)
   → A traffic spike or a slowdown affecting all services simultaneously.

NOTE: This chart combines all upstream services. To find which specific service is responsible, check the 'API Request Rate by Upstream Service' panel to the left — look for the service with rising 5xx counts or matching request timing.
panels.title: API Latency Distribution — all upstream services

panels.description:
One chart showing error signals from every major component. Any line that rises and stays non-zero needs investigation. Use it as a first triage step: identify which component is affected, then scroll to the matching section below for the root cause.

WHAT EACH LINE MEANS

Calcique errors/s
  The analytics engine failed to compute a query. Users will see a computation-failed or report-could-not-be-loaded error in the UI. A small transient spike during a first-time or complex report load can be normal. A sustained rate means something is consistently broken.
  Next step: Section 4c -- check Calcique Compute Latency and MAQL Phase Duration for overload, or SQL Executor duration if external data is involved.

Export failures/s
  A scheduled or on-demand export (PDF, Excel, CSV, PNG) failed to complete. The user receives an error notification instead of their file.
  Next step: Section 4d -- check Export Failure Rate by format to identify which export type is affected. For visual/PDF failures also check the Browser Pool panel.

Pulsar exceptions/s
  The internal message queue encountered an error. Pulsar handles background jobs (exports, scheduled emails, cache invalidation). Exceptions here can delay or silently drop background tasks without immediately showing up as user-facing errors.
  Next step: Section 4a -- check the Pulsar Unacknowledged Message Backlog. A growing backlog alongside exceptions means consumers are falling behind.

Pod restart rate
  One or more service pods crashed and restarted. A single restart after a deployment is normal. A continuous non-zero rate means a service is stuck in a crash loop and requests to it may be failing intermittently.
  Next step: Section 3 OOM Kills and Pod Restarts table -- find which container is restarting and whether it was OOM-killed (memory limit too low) or crashed for another reason. Check Section 5 logs for the crash cause.
panels.title: Application Error Rates (All Components)

panels.description:
Memory currently consumed by each container (working set bytes = heap + stack + OS page cache in active use). Sorted by % Requests descending so the most memory-hungry containers appear first.

% Requests -- how close the container is to its Kubernetes memory request. Above 90% means it is close to its scheduled allocation; node pressure may cause pod eviction.
% Limits -- how close the container is to its hard memory limit. Reaching 100% triggers an OOM Kill: Kubernetes forcibly restarts the container with no warning.

Last = current value. Avg and Max are computed over the selected time window.
panels.title: Container Memory Usage — Last · Avg · Max · % Requests · % Limits

panels.description: Peak CPU per service (max across replicas, not avg). Catches a single saturated pod masked by averaging.
panels.title: CPU Usage - Max per Service (% of requests)

panels.description: Peak CPU per service (max across replicas, not avg). Catches a single saturated pod masked by averaging.

targets.expr:
max(
    increase(container_cpu_cfs_throttled_periods_total{cluster_name=~"$cluster", image!="", container!="POD", namespace=~"$ns"}[$__rate_interval])
    /
    increase(container_cpu_cfs_periods_total{cluster_name=~"$cluster", image!="", container!="POD", namespace=~"$ns"}[$__rate_interval])
) by (container) * 100

panels.title: CPU Throtling - per Service

panels.description: Services with restarts or OOM kills in the selected time range. Restarts = counter delta (max–min over range) — catches pods that restarted and were replaced mid-window. OOM Kills = currently OOM-killed containers (sticky metric). OOM Pod = which pod was last OOM-killed.
panels.title: OOM Kills & Pod Restarts — by Service

panels.description: Message send/ack rates per service (Pulsar client metrics from GoodData service pods). Exceptions indicate connectivity or serialization problems with the Pulsar broker.
panels.title: Pulsar - Message Rates

panels.description: Unacknowledged message backlog per topic. A growing backlog means consumers are falling behind — correlate with pod restarts or Calcique/SQL Executor slowness.
panels.title: Pulsar - Unacknowledged Message Backlog

panels.description: Role of each Redis pod over time. Primary = active write node; Replica = standby.

targets.expr:
max by (pod) (
  (redis_instance_info{cluster_name=~"$cluster|", namespace=~"$ns", role="slave"} * 1)
  or
  (redis_instance_info{cluster_name=~"$cluster|", namespace=~"$ns", role="master"} * 0)
)

panels.title: Redis Replica Status

panels.description:
GoodData CN stores all internal metadata (workspaces, dashboards, data source connections, user permissions, scheduled exports) in a PostgreSQL database. This is a critical dependency -- if the metadata database is unreachable, the entire GoodData CN platform stops working.

DEPLOYMENT NOTE
This database is typically a cloud-managed service (AWS RDS, Azure Database for PostgreSQL, Google Cloud SQL) or deployed via the CloudNativePG operator on Kubernetes. GoodData CN does not manage the database -- you are responsible for backups, high availability, and disk space monitoring.

WHAT IS SHOWN
  PG active (datname ~ md.*) -- Active query connections to GoodData metadata databases. Should be stable and proportional to the number of running GoodData services.
  Hikari active -- Java connection pool connections currently executing a query inside each GoodData service.
  Hikari max -- The configured connection pool ceiling per service.

WHAT TO WATCH FOR
  Hikari active approaching Hikari max: Pool saturation. New requests queue waiting for a free connection, causing slow page loads and eventual API timeouts. Raise the pool ceiling or investigate slow queries holding connections open.
  PG active connections growing unbounded: Possible connection leak or long-running queries. Check the PostgreSQL slow query log.
  PG active connections drop suddenly to zero: The metadata database may be unreachable or has restarted. GoodData CN services will begin returning errors immediately.

For deeper PostgreSQL health (replication lag, disk usage, deadlocks, backup status) open the dedicated CloudNativePG dashboard from the GoodData CN Dashboards dropdown at the top of this page.
panels.title: Metadata Database — PostgreSQL & Hikari Connection Pools
4d — Analytics Engine | Calcique · SQL Executor · Result Cache

panels.description: Average and peak duration split by export type. Sustained high visual peak = headless Chromium rendering slow or browser pool exhausted. Sustained high tabular avg = external data source or large data volume issue.

targets.expr: sum by (EXPORT_TYPE) (rate(export_duration_seconds_sum{cluster_name=~"$cluster", namespace=~"$ns"}[$__rate_interval])) / sum by (EXPORT_TYPE) (rate(export_duration_seconds_count{cluster_name=~"$cluster", namespace=~"$ns"}[$__rate_interval]))

panels.title: Export Duration — avg & peak by type

panels.description: Headless Chromium browser pool used for PDF/PNG visual exports. Pool exhaustion (active = total, zero idle, rising acquire time) will queue and eventually fail all visual export requests.
panels.title: Visual Export — Browser Pool (export-builder)

panels.description: Count of 5xx upstream responses from the API gateway (forward_call_response_status_count_total). Bars let you spot exactly when errors started.
panels.title: Gateway 5xx Error Count

panels.description: Rate of ERROR and WARN log lines per container. Any sustained spike points to which service to investigate. Uses raw line filter so it works regardless of log format.
panels.title: Application ERROR/WARN Log Rate by Service

panels.description: API gateway (api-gw) log lines for 5xx upstream responses. Click a line to expand details — includes requestID and upstream service for quick diagnosis.
panels.title: API Gateway Error Logs

panels.description: ERROR and WARN log lines from Calcique (compute engine) and SQL Executor. Look for timeout/connection refused/exception messages to diagnose compute failures or external DB connectivity issues.
panels.title: Calcique & SQL-Executor Error Logs

panels.description: ERROR and WARN log lines from the export pipeline. Look for browser crash, timeout, template rendering failures, or storage errors.
panels.title: Export Pipeline Error Logs

panels.description: Requests sent to the Calcique analytics engine per minute and API-boundary errors. Rising errors with steady rate = compute failures; falling rate + rising errors = service overload. Correlate with Compute Latency panel.
panels.title: Calcique — Request Rate & API Errors

panels.description: Average end-to-end compute latency per request and peak (max) seen across any single request. Sustained high peak with low avg = individual slow queries. Both rising = systemic overload. Does NOT include metadata lookup time.
panels.title: Calcique — Compute Latency (avg & peak)

panels.description: SQL query duration against external customer databases. Avg/max duration and parallel query count. Sustained high parallelism with rising duration = external DB is saturated.
panels.title: SQL Executor - Duration & Parallelism

panels.description: Hikari connection pools used by SQL Executor to connect to external customer databases (sqlxhikaricp_*). Pool saturation (active ≈ max) = queries are queueing waiting for a connection.
panels.title: External Datasource Connection Pools (sqlx)

panels.description: Result cache hit ratio. Low hit ratio forces full recomputation in Calcique and more SQL queries against external databases. Miss breakdown shows which action type is causing misses.
panels.title: Result Cache - Hit Ratio & Misses

panels.description: Rate of failed exports per second, broken down by EXPORT_TYPE (tabular/visual) and EXPORT_FORMAT (CSV/XLSX/HTML/PDF). Any non-zero value means user export requests are failing. Correlate with SQL Executor (tabular) or Browser Pool (visual) panels below.
panels.title: Export Failure Rate — by type & format

panels.description: Total bytes of successfully generated export files per interval, by type and format. Spikes indicate large export requests that may be causing high memory/CPU pressure on tabular-exporter or visual-exporter-service. Useful for diagnosing slow export cycles caused by data volume rather than service issues.
panels.title: Export File Size — by type & format

title: GoodData CN - Overall Health
